# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

### **Q1. Define Simulated Annealing. State any two differences between Simulated Annealing and Gradient Descent.**

**Model Answer (≈ 12 lines):**

Simulated Annealing is a **probabilistic metaheuristic optimization algorithm** inspired by the physical annealing process in metallurgy. It uses a temperature parameter $T$ that is gradually decreased, allowing the algorithm to probabilistically accept worse solutions (via the Metropolis criterion) to escape local optima.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Simulated Annealing | Gradient Descent |
|---|---|---|
| Search nature | Stochastic / probabilistic | Deterministic |
| Local optima | Can escape using $e^{-\Delta E/T}$ | Always trapped |
| Derivative required | No | Yes |
| Temperature parameter | Yes | No |

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for any two crisp differences.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

### **Q2. What is the role of the cooling schedule in Simulated Annealing? List two commonly used cooling schedules.**

**Model Answer:**

The cooling schedule controls **how the temperature $T$ decreases** over iterations, thereby regulating the trade-off between **exploration** (high $T$, broad search) and **exploitation** (low $T$, refinement). An inappropriate cooling schedule — too fast → premature convergence to a local optimum; too slow → wasted computation.

**Two common schedules:**
1. **Geometric:** $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$, typically $\alpha \in [0.9,\, 0.99]$.
2. **Logarithmic (Geman–Geman):** $T_k = C / \log(k+1)$, theoretically guarantees global convergence.

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks role + 1.5 marks for the two schedules.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Most students **lose 3–4 marks** in SA numericals by forgetting to (a) update the temperature **after** each iteration block, not during, (b) distinguish between **accept with probability 1** and **accept with probability $e^{-\Delta E/T}$**, and (c) state the **random number comparison** explicitly when rejecting a worse move.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a)** Derive the Metropolis acceptance criterion $P_{\text{acc}} = e^{-\Delta E / T}$ starting from the Boltzmann distribution. State the assumption of detailed balance clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Minimize $f(x) = (x-4)^2$ over integer $x \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 8\}$. Use SA with $x^{(0)} = 0$, $T_0 = 5$, geometric cooling $\alpha = 0.5$, neighbourhood $\mathcal{N}(x) = \{x-1, x+1\}$ clipped to the feasible set, and random numbers $r = 0.30,\, 0.70,\, 0.20,\, 0.90,\, 0.50$ for uphill moves. Run **6 iterations** and report the best solution found. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

**Step 1 — Boltzmann distribution** at temperature $T$:
$$
P_{\text{eq}}(E_i) \;=\; \frac{1}{Z(T)} \, e^{-E_i / T}, \qquad Z(T) = \sum_j e^{-E_j / T}
$$

**Step 2 — Ratio of probabilities** between current state $x$ and candidate $x'$:
$$
\frac{P_{\text{eq}}(x')}{P_{\text{eq}}(x)} \;=\; \frac{(1/Z)\,e^{-f(x')/T}}{(1/Z)\,e^{-f(x)/T}} \;=\; e^{-(f(x')-f(x))/T} \;=\; e^{-\Delta E / T}
$$

**Step 3 — Detailed balance** (Markov chain equilibrium condition):
$$
P_{\text{eq}}(x) \cdot P(x \to x') \;=\; P_{\text{eq}}(x') \cdot P(x' \to x)
$$
Assuming **symmetric proposal**: $P(x \to x') = P(x' \to x)$, the proposal terms cancel and we directly obtain the equilibrium ratio $e^{-\Delta E / T}$.

**Step 4 — Acceptance rule** ensuring detailed balance:
$$
P_{\text{acc}}(x \to x') \;=\; \min\!\Bigl\{\, 1,\; e^{-\Delta E / T} \,\Bigr\}
$$
- If $\Delta E \leq 0 \Rightarrow P_{\text{acc}} = 1$ (always accept improvements).
- If $\Delta E > 0 \Rightarrow P_{\text{acc}} = e^{-\Delta E / T}$ (probabilistic uphill move). $\blacksquare$

*(7 marks: 2 distribution + 2 detailed balance + 2 final rule + 1 conclusion.)*

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

Pre-compute: $f(x) = (x-4)^2$. Values: $f(0)=16,\,f(1)=9,\,f(2)=4,\,f(3)=1,\,f(4)=0,\,f(5)=1,\,f(6)=4,\,f(7)=9,\,f(8)=16$.

| Iter $k$ | $T_k$ | $x$ | $f(x)$ | $x'$ | $f(x')$ | $\Delta E$ | $P_{\text{acc}}$ | $r$ | Decision | $x_{\text{new}}$ |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|:---:|:---:|---:|
| 1 | 5.0 | 0 | 16 | 1 | 9 | $-7$ | $1$ | — | **accept** | 1 |
| 2 | 5.0 | 1 | 9 | 2 | 4 | $-5$ | $1$ | — | **accept** | 2 |
| 3 | 5.0 | 2 | 4 | 3 | 1 | $-3$ | $1$ | — | **accept** | 3 |
| 4 | 5.0 | 3 | 1 | 2 | 4 | $+3$ | $e^{-3/5}=0.5488$ | 0.30 | **accept** | 2 |
| 5 | 5.0 | 2 | 4 | 3 | 1 | $-3$ | $1$ | — | **accept** | 3 |
| 6 | 5.0 | 3 | 1 | 4 | 0 | $-1$ | $1$ | — | **accept** | 4 |

After iteration 6: $T_{7} = 0.5 \times 5 = 2.5$ (cooling applied at end).

**Best solution:** $x^{*} = 4$, $f(x^{*}) = 0$. $\blacksquare$

*(7 marks: 1 trace setup + 4 iteration table + 1 cooling note + 1 final answer.)*

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Discuss **any four cooling schedules** used in Simulated Annealing. For each, state one advantage and one limitation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) with 4 cities A, B, C, D and the distance matrix below. Apply **two iterations** of SA starting from tour $T_0 = (A \to B \to C \to D \to A)$ with $T_0 = 100$. Use **2-opt swap** as neighbourhood (swap any two non-adjacent edges) and $\alpha = 0.5$ for cooling. Use random numbers $r = 0.95$ and $r = 0.50$ for uphill decisions. Report the best tour and cost. **(7 marks)**

$$
\text{Distance Matrix } D = 
\begin{pmatrix}
0 & 10 & 15 & 20 \\
10 & 0 & 35 & 25 \\
15 & 35 & 0 & 30 \\
20 & 25 & 30 & 0
\end{pmatrix}
$$

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

| Schedule | Formula | Advantage | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Geometric** | $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$ | Simple, widely used, controllable via $\alpha$ | No convergence guarantee for finite $\alpha$; $T$ never reaches $0$ exactly |
| **Linear** | $T_{k+1} = T_k - \beta$ | Easy to implement; reaches $0$ | May cool too fast, causing premature convergence |
| **Logarithmic (Geman–Geman)** | $T_k = C / \log(k+1)$ | Theoretically guarantees global convergence | Extremely slow in practice; rarely used directly |
| **Adaptive** | $T_{k+1}$ depends on acceptance rate | Self-tunes to problem landscape | More parameters; harder to analyse theoretically |

*(7 marks: 4 × (formula 0.5 + advantage 0.75 + limitation 0.5) ≈ 7 marks.)*

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

**Step 1 — Initial tour cost.** Tour $(A\to B\to C\to D\to A)$:
$$
d(A,B)+d(B,C)+d(C,D)+d(D,A) = 10+35+30+20 = 95
$$

**Step 2 — Iteration 1.** Try 2-opt swap between edges (A,B) and (C,D). New tour: $(A\to C\to B\to D\to A)$.
$$
\text{Cost} = d(A,C)+d(C,B)+d(B,D)+d(D,A) = 15+35+25+20 = 95
$$
$\Delta E = 0 \Rightarrow$ accepted deterministically. New tour = $(A\to C\to B\to D\to A)$, cost $95$.

**Step 3 — Cooling.** $T_1 = 0.5 \times 100 = 50$.

**Step 4 — Iteration 2.** From current tour, try swapping edges (A,C) and (B,D). New tour: $(A\to D\to B\to C\to A)$.

Wait — a 2-opt swap from $(A\to C\to B\to D\to A)$ exchanging the (A,C) and (B,D) connections produces $(A\to B\to D\to C\to A)$.

Recompute:
$$
d(A,B)+d(B,D)+d(D,C)+d(C,A) = 10+25+30+15 = 80
$$

$\Delta E = 80 - 95 = -15 < 0 \Rightarrow$ **accept deterministically**.

**Best tour:** $(A \to B \to D \to C \to A)$, **best cost = 80**. $\blacksquare$

*(7 marks: 1 initial cost + 1 iter 1 swap + 1 cooling + 2 iter 2 swap + 1 cost + 1 conclusion.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — 2-opt Swap Clarity:** A common mistake is to *describe* the swap without *recomputing the tour edges explicitly*. KTU examiners award marks only when the **new edge list** is written and **each edge cost is summed** separately. Always show: original edges → new edges → summed cost.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Simulated Annealing**

- **Origin:** Kirkpatrick, Gelatt & Vecchi (1983), inspired by Metropolis et al. (1953).
- **Core idea:** Probabilistically accept worse solutions at high $T$, greedily at low $T$.
- **Acceptance rule:** $P_{\text{acc}} = \min\{1,\, e^{-\Delta E/T}\}$.
- **Always accept** improvements ($\Delta E \leq 0$).
- **Geometric cooling** $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$ with $\alpha \in [0.9, 0.99]$ is the **most common** in practice.
- **Logarithmic cooling** $T_k = C/\log(k+1)$ is **theoretically optimal** (Geman–Geman) but impractically slow.
- **Convergence guarantee** holds only under sufficiently slow cooling — fast cooling = local optimum risk.
- **Neighbourhood design** is problem-specific: 1-bit flip for binary, 2-opt swap for TSP, single-coordinate perturbation for continuous.
- **Single-solution memory** distinguishes SA from **population-based** GAs.
- **No derivatives required** — SA is a **direct search / black-box** method.
- **Typical tuning parameters:** $T_0$, $\alpha$, neighbourhood size, $T_{\min}$, max iterations.
- **Boltzmann constant $k_B$** is **absorbed into $T$** in the SA formulation — never multiply by $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ in exam answers unless explicitly asked.
- **In trace tables:** always show $\Delta E$, $P_{\text{acc}}$, random number $r$, decision, **and** the new state on every iteration.
- **KTU keyword triggers:** "metallurgy", "Metropolis criterion", "Boltzmann", "cooling schedule", "local optimum escape", "detailed balance".

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 - Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
**Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)** is a population-based, stochastic, meta-heuristic optimization algorithm inspired by the social behaviour of bird flocking and fish schooling. Proposed by **James Kennedy and Russell Eberhart (1995)**, PSO solves a problem by iteratively improving a candidate solution (called a *particle*) with regard to a given measure of quality (the *fitness function*). Each particle moves through the search space with a velocity that is dynamically adjusted according to its own historical best position (**pbest**) and the global best position discovered by the swarm (**gbest**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> PSO is treated as a *Nature-Inspired / Swarm Intelligence* algorithm under Module 4 (Meta-heuristic Optimization Techniques). It is a *zero-order* method, i.e., it does **not** require gradient information of the objective function, making it suitable for non-differentiable, discontinuous, and black-box optimization problems.

---

### Conceptual Analogy (The Bird Flock Story)
Imagine a flock of birds searching for a hidden piece of food in a wide field, but **only one bird at a time can sense how close it is to the food** (a fitness feedback). The strategy the flock uses mirrors PSO precisely:

- Each bird = a **Particle** (a candidate solution $x_i$).
- Each bird's current location = the particle's **position** in the search space.
- Each bird's current flying speed & direction = the particle's **velocity** $v_i$.
- The best location that **a single bird** has ever visited = **pbest** (personal best).
- The best location **any bird in the flock** has ever visited = **gbest** (global best).
- The field = the **search/feasible space** of the problem.

Each bird therefore flies in a direction that is a balanced combination of:
1. **Its own memory** (return to where it personally did well).
2. **Social learning** (move toward the best location found by the group).
3. **Momentum** (continue along its current trajectory, governed by *inertia*).

Over many such adjustments, the entire swarm converges to the food source (the optimum).

---

### Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Particle | $i$ | A single candidate solution vector $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| Swarm | $S$ | The complete population of $N$ particles |
| Position | $\mathbf{x}_i(t)$ | Current location of particle $i$ at iteration $t$ |
| Velocity | $\mathbf{v}_i(t)$ | Current displacement vector of particle $i$ |
| Personal best | $\mathbf{p}_i$ | Best position ever attained by particle $i$ |
| Global best | $\mathbf{g}$ | Best position ever attained by **any** particle in the swarm |
| Inertia weight | $w$ | Controls the influence of previous velocity |
| Cognitive coefficient | $c_1$ | Pull toward pbest (self-experience) |
| Social coefficient | $c_2$ | Pull toward gbest (group experience) |
| Random vectors | $r_1, r_2$ | Uniformly distributed in $[0,1]$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Two important topological variants** exist in PSO:
> - **Global Best (gbest) PSO** — every particle is influenced by the *single* best particle of the entire swarm. Faster convergence, but more prone to local optima.
> - **Local Best (lbest) PSO** — every particle is influenced only by the *best among a small neighbourhood*. Slower but more diverse search.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Canonical PSO Algorithm — Operational Logic

The PSO algorithm proceeds in a structured, repetitive cycle. Each iteration updates every particle's velocity and position based on three components.

### Step-by-Step Logic of a Single Iteration

1. **Initialization (Iteration $t = 0$):**
   - Randomly initialize positions $\mathbf{x}_i(0)$ within bounds $[\mathbf{x}_{\min}, \mathbf{x}_{\max}]$.
   - Randomly initialize velocities $\mathbf{v}_i(0)$, typically $\mathbf{v}_i(0) \in [-V_{\max}, V_{\max}]$.
   - Set $\mathbf{p}_i(0) = \mathbf{x}_i(0)$ (a particle's first position is its first pbest).
   - Identify $\mathbf{g}(0) = \arg\min_{i} f(\mathbf{x}_i(0))$ (best among all particles).

2. **Fitness Evaluation:**
   - Compute $f(\mathbf{x}_i(t))$ for every particle $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$.

3. **Update Personal Best (pbest):**
   - If $f(\mathbf{x}_i(t)) < f(\mathbf{p}_i)$, then $\mathbf{p}_i \leftarrow \mathbf{x}_i(t)$. (For minimization.)

4. **Update Global Best (gbest):**
   - If $f(\mathbf{p}_i) < f(\mathbf{g})$, then $\mathbf{g} \leftarrow \mathbf{p}_i$.

5. **Update Velocity (the heart of PSO):**
   $$\mathbf{v}_i(t+1) = w\,\mathbf{v}_i(t) + c_1 r_1 \big(\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{x}_i(t)\big) + c_2 r_2 \big(\mathbf{g} - \mathbf{x}_i(t)\big)$$

6. **Update Position:**
   $$\mathbf{x}_i(t+1) = \mathbf{x}_i(t) + \mathbf{v}_i(t+1)$$

7. **Bound Enforcement (optional but standard):**
   - Clamp $\mathbf{x}_i(t+1) \in [\mathbf{x}_{\min}, \mathbf{x}_{\max}]$.
   - Clamp $\mathbf{v}_i(t+1) \in [-V_{\max}, V_{\max}]$ to prevent divergence.

8. **Termination Check:**
   - If a stopping criterion is met (max iterations, tolerance on $f(\mathbf{g})$, or stall counter), stop. Else $t \leftarrow t+1$ and go to Step 2.

---

## 2.2 The Three Components of the Velocity Update — The "Why"

The velocity vector is a **weighted sum of three distinct influences**. Understanding each is essential for KTU long-answer questions.

### Component 1: The Inertia Term $w\,\mathbf{v}_i(t)$
- **What:** Carries forward the particle's velocity from the previous step.
- **Why:** Provides **momentum** so the particle does not abruptly change direction. It is the *exploitation* engine that lets the particle keep travelling along a promising trajectory.
- **Tuning:** A large $w$ promotes global exploration; a small $w$ promotes local exploitation. A common schedule is **linearly decreasing inertia**, e.g. $w(t) = w_{\max} - (w_{\max} - w_{\min})\dfrac{t}{T_{\max}}$, with $w_{\max}=0.9$, $w_{\min}=0.4$.

### Component 2: The Cognitive Term $c_1 r_1 (\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{x}_i(t))$
- **What:** Pulls the particle back toward its own best-known location.
- **Why:** Models **individual memory / self-confidence**. Without this term, the swarm would have no individual learning, only herd behaviour.
- **Tuning:** $c_1 \in [0,2]$ typically; $c_1 = 1.5$ is a common default.

### Component 3: The Social Term $c_2 r_2 (\mathbf{g} - \mathbf{x}_i(t))$
- **What:** Pulls the particle toward the best position found by the swarm.
- **Why:** Models **group learning / social influence**. This is what enables emergent collective intelligence.
- **Tuning:** $c_2 \in [0,2]$ typically; $c_2 = 1.5$ is a common default.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Symbol | Description | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|------------------|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $\mathbf{v}_i(t+1) = w\,\mathbf{v}_i(t) + c_1 r_1 (\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{x}_i(t)) + c_2 r_2 (\mathbf{g} - \mathbf{x}_i(t))$ | Canonical velocity update | — |
| 2 | $\mathbf{x}_i(t+1) = \mathbf{x}_i(t) + \mathbf{v}_i(t+1)$ | Position update | — |
| 3 | $w$ | Inertia weight (constant or time-varying) | $0.4 \le w \le 0.9$ |
| 4 | $c_1$ | Cognitive acceleration coefficient | $1.5$ (typical) |
| 5 | $c_2$ | Social acceleration coefficient | $1.5$ (typical) |
| 6 | $r_1, r_2$ | Uniformly distributed random numbers | $r_1, r_2 \sim U(0,1)$ |
| 7 | $w(t) = w_{\max} - (w_{\max} - w_{\min})\,\dfrac{t}{T_{\max}}$ | Linearly decreasing inertia schedule | $w_{\max}=0.9$, $w_{\min}=0.4$ |
| 8 | $\mathbf{x}_i \in [\mathbf{x}_{\min}, \mathbf{x}_{\max}]$ | Position boundary | Problem-specific |
| 9 | $v_{ij} \in [-V_{\max}, V_{\max}]$ | Velocity clamp | Often $V_{\max}=0.2\,(x_{\max}-x_{\min})$ |
| 10 | $N$ (swarm size) | Number of particles | $20$–$50$ is standard |
| 11 | $\phi = c_1 + c_2$ | Acceleration constant | For convergence, $\phi \ge 4$ |
| 12 | Constriction coefficient: $\chi = \dfrac{2}{\phi - 2 + \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi}}$ | Clerc's constriction method | $\phi = 4.1 \Rightarrow \chi \approx 0.729$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of PSO:**
> - **Power systems** — economic dispatch, unit commitment, optimal power flow.
> - **Control engineering** — PID tuning, controller parameter optimization.
> - **Structural engineering** — truss weight minimization, topology optimization.
> - **Machine learning** — neural network weight (hyperparameter) tuning.
> - **Image & signal processing** — feature selection, thresholding, filter design.

---

## 2.4 Convergence Behaviour — A Key Theoretical Insight

For the PSO dynamics to guarantee convergence (in the sense of particles forming a stable distribution around a point), the literature establishes that when $w$, $c_1$, $c_2$ are **constant**, the trajectory of a particle remains bounded provided:

$$0 \le w < 1, \qquad 0 < c_1 + c_2 < 4(1 + w)$$

If $c_1 + c_2 \ge 4$, the particle's position amplitude grows without bound — the swarm **explodes**. This is why the inertia weight and the acceleration coefficients must be carefully chosen.

A more robust approach is **Clerc & Kennedy's constriction coefficient method**, which automatically guarantees convergence by replacing the three parameters with a single $\chi$:

$$\mathbf{v}_i(t+1) = \chi\!\left[\mathbf{v}_i(t) + c_1 r_1 (\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{x}_i(t)) + c_2 r_2 (\mathbf{g} - \mathbf{x}_i(t))\right]$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Velocity Update Equation — From a Spring-Damper Analogy

The canonical PSO update can be **interpreted** as a discrete-time second-order dynamical system. Consider a single particle of "position" $x$ and "velocity" $v$ subject to two virtual springs:

- One spring with stiffness $k_1$ connecting the particle to its own pbest $p$.
- Another spring with stiffness $k_2$ connecting the particle to the global best $g$.
- A damper that dissipates the velocity with coefficient $\gamma$.

The continuous-time equation of motion (per dimension) is:

$$m\,\ddot{x} = -k_1 (x - p) - k_2 (x - g) - \gamma \dot{x}$$

Setting $m = 1$ and rearranging:

$$\ddot{x} + \gamma \dot{x} + (k_1 + k_2) x = k_1 p + k_2 g$$

Define the constants $\alpha = k_1 + k_2$ and $\beta = \gamma$. The discrete-time Euler approximation of this ODE, with step size $\Delta t = 1$, gives:

$$v(t+1) = v(t) - \beta\,v(t) + c_1(p - x(t)) + c_2(g - x(t))$$

where we identify the cognitive pull $c_1 = k_1$ and the social pull $c_2 = k_2$. Defining $w = 1 - \beta$, we obtain the canonical PSO update:

$$\boxed{\,v(t+1) = w\,v(t) + c_1(p - x(t)) + c_2(g - x(t))\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation (introduced by Clerc & Kennedy, 2002) shows that PSO is **not just a heuristic** — it has a well-defined dynamical-systems interpretation. This is a frequently asked conceptual question in KTU ESE.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Minimizing a 1-D Function via PSO

### Problem Statement
> Minimize $f(x) = x^2$ over $x \in \mathbb{R}$, using PSO with **3 particles**. The analytical optimum is at $x^* = 0$, $f(x^*) = 0$.

### Given / Chosen Parameters
- Swarm size $N = 3$
- Inertia weight $w = 0.7$
- Cognitive coefficient $c_1 = 1.5$
- Social coefficient $c_2 = 1.5$
- Random numbers: assume $r_1 = r_2 = 0.5$ for all particles and all iterations (deterministic for illustration)
- Initial state (iteration $t = 0$):

| Particle | $x_i(0)$ | $v_i(0)$ | $f(x_i(0)) = x_i^2$ |
|----------|-----------|-----------|----------------------|
| P1 | 5 | 0 | 25 |
| P2 | $-3$ | 0 | 9 |
| P3 | 2 | 0 | 4 |

- **Initial pbest positions:** $p_1 = 5$, $p_2 = -3$, $p_3 = 2$ (each particle's first position)
- **Initial gbest:** $g(0) = \arg\min\{25, 9, 4\} = 2$ (P3 has the smallest fitness)

---

### Iteration 1 ($t = 0 \to t = 1$)

#### Particle P1
Velocity update:

$$v_1(1) = (0.7)(0) + (1.5)(0.5)(p_1 - x_1(0)) + (1.5)(0.5)(g(0) - x_1(0))$$

$$v_1(1) = 0 + 0.75(5 - 5) + 0.75(2 - 5)$$

$$v_1(1) = 0 + 0.75(0) + 0.75(-3)$$

$$v_1(1) = 0 + 0 - 2.25 = -2.25$$

Position update:

$$x_1(1) = x_1(0) + v_1(1) = 5 + (-2.25) = 2.75$$

New fitness: $f(x_1(1)) = (2.75)^2 = 7.5625$

Since $7.5625 < 25$, **pbest updates**: $p_1 \leftarrow 2.75$

#### Particle P2
Velocity update:

$$v_2(1) = (0.7)(0) + (1.5)(0.5)(p_2 - x_2(0)) + (1.5)(0.5)(g(0) - x_2(0))$$

$$v_2(1) = 0 + 0.75(-3 - (-3)) + 0.75(2 - (-3))$$

$$v_2(1) = 0 + 0.75(0) + 0.75(5)$$

$$v_2(1) = 0 + 0 + 3.75 = 3.75$$

Position update:

$$x_2(1) = x_2(0) + v_2(1) = -3 + 3.75 = 0.75$$

New fitness: $f(x_2(1)) = (0.75)^2 = 0.5625$

Since $0.5625 < 9$, **pbest updates**: $p_2 \leftarrow 0.75$

#### Particle P3
Velocity update:

$$v_3(1) = (0.7)(0) + (1.5)(0.5)(p_3 - x_3(0)) + (1.5)(0.5)(g(0) - x_3(0))$$

$$v_3(1) = 0 + 0.75(2 - 2) + 0.75(2 - 2)$$

$$v_3(1) = 0 + 0 + 0 = 0$$

Position update:

$$x_3(1) = x_3(0) + v_3(1) = 2 + 0 = 2$$

New fitness: $f(x_3(1)) = (2)^2 = 4$. **No improvement.** $p_3$ stays at $2$.

#### End of Iteration 1 — Update gbest
Best fitness values now: $7.5625$ (P1), $0.5625$ (P2), $4$ (P3).

Minimum is at **P2**: $g(1) \leftarrow 0.75$

**State after iteration 1:**

| Particle | $x_i(1)$ | $v_i(1)$ | $p_i$ | $f(x_i(1))$ |
|----------|-----------|-----------|--------|--------------|
| P1 | 2.75 | $-2.25$ | 2.75 | 7.5625 |
| P2 | 0.75 | 3.75 | 0.75 | **0.5625** |
| P3 | 2 | 0 | 2 | 4 |
| $g$ | — | — | **0.75** | **0.5625** |

---

### Iteration 2 ($t = 1 \to t = 2$)

#### Particle P1
$$v_1(2) = (0.7)(-2.25) + (0.75)(p_1 - x_1(1)) + (0.75)(g(1) - x_1(1))$$

$$v_1(2) = -1.575 + 0.75(2.75 - 2.75) + 0.75(0.75 - 2.75)$$

$$v_1(2) = -1.575 + 0 + 0.75(-2) = -1.575 - 1.5 = -3.075$$

$$x_1(2) = 2.75 + (-3.075) = -0.325$$

$$f(x_1(2)) = (-0.325)^2 = 0.1056$$

Since $0.1056 < 7.5625$, **pbest updates**: $p_1 \leftarrow -0.325$

#### Particle P2
$$v_2(2) = (0.7)(3.75) + (0.75)(0.75 - 0.75) + (0.75)(0.75 - 0.75)$$

$$v_2(2) = 2.625 + 0 + 0 = 2.625$$

$$x_2(2) = 0.75 + 2.625 = 3.375$$

$$f(x_2(2)) = (3.375)^2 = 11.39$$

Since $11.39 > 0.5625$, **pbest does NOT update**: $p_2$ stays at $0.75$

#### Particle P3
$$v_3(2) = (0.7)(0) + (0.75)(2 - 2) + (0.75)(0.75 - 2)$$

$$v_3(2) = 0 + 0 + 0.75(-1.25) = -0.9375$$

$$x_3(2) = 2 + (-0.9375) = 1.0625$$

$$f(x_3(2)) = (1.0625)^2 = 1.1289$

Since $1.1289 > 4$? No, $1.1289 < 4$, so **pbest updates**: $p_3 \leftarrow 1.0625$

#### End of Iteration 2 — Update gbest
Best fitness values: $0.1056$ (P1), $0.5625$ (P2), $1.1289$ (P3).

Minimum is now at **P1**: $g(2) \leftarrow -0.325$, $f(g) = 0.1056$.

---

### Convergence Observation

| Iteration | $g$ (best position) | $f(g)$ |
|-----------|---------------------|--------|
| 0 | 2 | 4 |
| 1 | 0.75 | 0.5625 |
| 2 | $-0.325$ | 0.1056 |

The global best is **monotonically decreasing** and rapidly moving toward the true optimum $x^* = 0$. With stochastic $r_1, r_2$ (rather than the fixed 0.5 used here) and more iterations, the swarm will converge to within a small $\epsilon$ of the true optimum.

---

## 3.3 Generalization to n-Dimensional Search Space

For a particle in an $n$-dimensional search space, the update equations are applied **component-wise**:

$$v_{ij}(t+1) = w\,v_{ij}(t) + c_1 r_{1j}\!\left(p_{ij} - x_{ij}(t)\right) + c_2 r_{2j}\!\left(g_j - x_{ij}(t)\right)$$

$$x_{ij}(t+1) = x_{ij}(t) + v_{ij}(t+1), \qquad j = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

with $r_{1j}, r_{2j}$ being *independent* uniform random numbers for each dimension $j$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU answers:** When asked to "apply PSO to a function of two variables", treat each particle as $\mathbf{x}_i = (x_{i1}, x_{i2})$, and run the update on **both** coordinates. A common student error is to apply PSO only to one variable, losing half the problem.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the PSO Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1([START]) --> B1[/Initialize swarm: positions x_i, velocities v_i/]
    B1 --> C1[/Initialize pbest_i = x_i and gbest = argmin f/]
    C1 --> D1[/Evaluate fitness f x_i for all particles/]
    D1 --> E1{Is f x_i less than f pbest_i?}
    E1 -- YES --> F1[Update pbest_i = x_i]
    E1 -- NO --> G1[Keep pbest_i unchanged]
    F1 --> H1{Is f pbest_i less than f gbest?}
    G1 --> H1
    H1 -- YES --> I1[Update gbest = pbest_i]
    H1 -- NO --> J1[Keep gbest unchanged]
    I1 --> K1[Compute new velocity v_i t+1 using canonical equation]
    J1 --> K1
    K1 --> L1[Compute new position x_i t+1 = x_i t + v_i t+1]
    L1 --> M1[Clamp position and velocity to bounds]
    M1 --> N1{Termination criterion met?}
    N1 -- NO --> D1
    N1 -- YES --> O1[/Output gbest as optimal solution/]
    O1 --> P1([STOP])
```

---

## 4.2 Conceptual Diagram — Particle Movement Under Three Influences

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Particle at x_i t] -- Inertia w v_i t --> B2[New x_i t+1]
    A2 -- Cognitive pull c1 r1 p_i minus x_i t --> B2
    A2 -- Social pull c2 r2 g minus x_i t --> B2
    B2 --> C2{Improves fitness?}
    C2 -- YES --> D2[Update pbest and possibly gbest]
    C2 -- NO --> E2[Retain old pbest gbest]
```

---

## 4.3 Topological Comparison: gbest vs lbest PSO

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GlobalBest["GLOBAL BEST gbest PSO"]
        GB1[Particle 1] --- GB2[Particle 2]
        GB2 --- GB3[Particle 3]
        GB3 --- GB4[Particle 4]
        GB4 --- GB5[Global Star Hub gbest]
        GB1 --- GB5
    end
    subgraph LocalBest["LOCAL BEST lbest PSO"]
        LB1[Particle 1] --- LB2[Particle 2]
        LB2 --- LB3[Particle 3]
        LB3 --- LB4[Particle 4]
        LB4 --- LB5[Particle 5]
        LB1 --- LB6[Ring Topology lbest per neighborhood]
        LB3 --- LB6
        LB5 --- LB6
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams for KTU answers:**
> - The first diagram is the *algorithmic flowchart* you should reproduce in an exam when asked to "describe the PSO algorithm".
> - The second emphasizes the **three-force analogy** (momentum, cognitive, social) — useful for "explain the velocity update equation" type questions.
> - The third is for the *gbest vs lbest* comparison — a popular 7-mark question.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
**Define Particle Swarm Optimization. List any four parameters that control the behaviour of a PSO algorithm.**

**Model Answer:**
Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) is a population-based stochastic meta-heuristic algorithm inspired by the collective behaviour of bird flocking and fish schooling, in which a swarm of candidate solutions (particles) moves through the search space by adjusting their velocities based on their own best-known position (pbest) and the swarm's best-known position (gbest).

Four controlling parameters are:
1. **Inertia weight $w$** — controls momentum from the previous velocity.
2. **Cognitive coefficient $c_1$** — weight of the particle's own experience (pbest).
3. **Social coefficient $c_2$** — weight of the swarm's experience (gbest).
4. **Swarm size $N$** — number of particles in the population.

*(Any 4 valid parameters accepted.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
**Differentiate between pbest and gbest in PSO.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | pbest ($\mathbf{p}_i$) | gbest ($\mathbf{g}$) |
|--------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Scope | Local to a single particle $i$ | Global to the entire swarm |
| Update rule | Updated only if $f(\mathbf{x}_i) < f(\mathbf{p}_i)$ | Updated if **any** particle's pbest improves the global minimum |
| Represents | Particle's individual memory | Swarm's collective intelligence |
| Role in velocity update | Cognitive term: $c_1 r_1(\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{x}_i)$ | Social term: $c_2 r_2(\mathbf{g} - \mathbf{x}_i)$ |
| Number in a swarm of $N$ | $N$ values (one per particle) | Only 1 value |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the Particle Swarm Optimization algorithm in detail. State and explain the velocity and position update equations. Discuss the role of inertia weight, cognitive coefficient, and social coefficient. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Initialize** a swarm of $N$ particles with random positions $\mathbf{x}_i(0)$ and velocities $\mathbf{v}_i(0)$ within prescribed bounds.
2. **Evaluate** the fitness $f(\mathbf{x}_i)$ of every particle.
3. **Set pbest:** $\mathbf{p}_i = \mathbf{x}_i$ for all $i$.
4. **Set gbest:** $\mathbf{g} = \arg\min_i f(\mathbf{x}_i)$.
5. **Repeat** until stopping criterion is satisfied:
    - **Update pbest** for each particle: if $f(\mathbf{x}_i) < f(\mathbf{p}_i)$, set $\mathbf{p}_i = \mathbf{x}_i$.
    - **Update gbest:** if any $f(\mathbf{p}_i) < f(\mathbf{g})$, set $\mathbf{g} = \mathbf{p}_i$.
    - **Update velocity** for each particle $i$ and each dimension $j$:

$$\mathbf{v}_i(t+1) = w\,\mathbf{v}_i(t) + c_1 r_1 \big(\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{x}_i(t)\big) + c_2 r_2 \big(\mathbf{g} - \mathbf{x}_i(t)\big)$$

    - **Update position:** $\mathbf{x}_i(t+1) = \mathbf{x}_i(t) + \mathbf{v}_i(t+1)$.
    - **Apply boundary constraints** on $\mathbf{x}_i$ and $\mathbf{v}_i$.
6. **Output** the final gbest as the optimal solution.

**Role of parameters:**

- **Inertia weight $w$:** Controls how much of the previous velocity is retained. A high $w$ ($\approx 0.9$) favours global exploration; a low $w$ ($\approx 0.4$) favours local exploitation. Linearly decreasing $w$ from $0.9$ to $0.4$ over the run is a proven good strategy.
- **Cognitive coefficient $c_1$:** Quantifies the particle's trust in its own past performance. Drives individual learning.
- **Social coefficient $c_2$:** Quantifies the particle's trust in the swarm's best. Drives collective learning. The balance $c_1 \approx c_2 \approx 1.5$ is widely used.

#### (b) Solve the following minimization problem using one full iteration of PSO:

$$\min f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 5$$

Use $N = 3$ particles with initial positions $x_1(0) = 0$, $x_2(0) = 4$, $x_3(0) = 1$; initial velocities $v_1(0) = 1$, $v_2(0) = -1$, $v_3(0) = 0.5$; parameters $w = 0.5$, $c_1 = c_2 = 1.5$, $r_1 = r_2 = 0.5$. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The objective is $f(x) = (x-2)^2 + 1$, with analytical optimum at $x^* = 2$, $f(2) = 1$.

**Step 1 — Initial fitness:**
- $f(x_1) = 0^2 - 0 + 5 = 5$
- $f(x_2) = 16 - 16 + 5 = 5$
- $f(x_3) = 1 - 4 + 5 = 2$

**Step 2 — Initial pbest and gbest:**
- $p_1 = 0$, $p_2 = 4$, $p_3 = 1$
- $g(0) = 1$ (P3 has the lowest fitness = 2)

**Step 3 — Velocity update for P1:**

$$v_1(1) = (0.5)(1) + (1.5)(0.5)(0 - 0) + (1.5)(0.5)(1 - 0)$$

$$v_1(1) = 0.5 + 0 + 0.75 = 1.25$$

$$x_1(1) = 0 + 1.25 = 1.25, \quad f(1.25) = 1.5625 - 5 + 5 = 1.5625$$

Since $1.5625 < 5$, **update** $p_1 \leftarrow 1.25$.

**Step 4 — Velocity update for P2:**

$$v_2(1) = (0.5)(-1) + (0.75)(4 - 4) + (0.75)(1 - 4)$$

$$v_2(1) = -0.5 + 0 + 0.75(-3) = -0.5 - 2.25 = -2.75$$

$$x_2(1) = 4 + (-2.75) = 1.25, \quad f(1.25) = 1.5625$$

Since $1.5625 < 5$, **update** $p_2 \leftarrow 1.25$.

**Step 5 — Velocity update for P3:**

$$v_3(1) = (0.5)(0.5) + (0.75)(1 - 1) + (0.75)(1 - 1) = 0.25 + 0 + 0 = 0.25$$

$$x_3(1) = 1 + 0.25 = 1.25, \quad f(1.25) = 1.5625$$

Since $1.5625 < 2$, **update** $p_3 \leftarrow 1.25$.

**Step 6 — Update gbest:**
- New fitness: $1.5625$ (P1), $1.5625$ (P2), $1.5625$ (P3) — all equal.
- $g(1) = 1.25$ (any one of the three; conventionally the lowest-indexed), $f(g) = 1.5625$.

**Result after one iteration:** $g(1) = 1.25$, $f(g) = 1.5625$. The swarm has moved from $f = 2$ at $x = 1$ to $f = 1.5625$ at $x = 1.25$, clearly converging toward the true optimum $x^* = 2$.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) Discuss the role of inertia weight, cognitive coefficient, and social coefficient in PSO. Explain the linearly decreasing inertia weight (LDIW) strategy and the constriction coefficient approach. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Inertia Weight $w$:**
The term $w \mathbf{v}_i(t)$ carries over the particle's previous velocity, giving it *momentum*. A large $w$ allows the particle to make large jumps (good for early exploration). A small $w$ restricts movement (good for late exploitation). A commonly cited bound is $w \in [0.4, 0.9]$.

**2. Cognitive Coefficient $c_1$:**
The cognitive term $c_1 r_1 (\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{x}_i)$ pulls each particle toward its own best position. If $c_1 = 0$, the particle has *no memory* — it becomes a purely social agent and may converge prematurely. Typical $c_1 = 1.5$.

**3. Social Coefficient $c_2$:**
The social term $c_2 r_2 (\mathbf{g} - \mathbf{x}_i)$ pulls the particle toward the swarm's best. If $c_2 = 0$, the particle becomes purely cognitive — each roams independently and the swarm fails to converge. Typical $c_2 = 1.5$.

**4. Linearly Decreasing Inertia Weight (LDIW):**
Proposed by Shi & Eberhart, this strategy adaptively tunes $w$ over the run:

$$w(t) = w_{\max} - \frac{(w_{\max} - w_{\min}) \cdot t}{T_{\max}}$$

with $w_{\max} = 0.9$, $w_{\min} = 0.4$. Early iterations (large $w$) emphasize exploration; later iterations (small $w$) emphasize exploitation. This simple schedule has been shown to **significantly improve convergence speed and solution quality**.

**5. Constriction Coefficient Method (Clerc & Kennedy, 2002):**
This is an alternative parameterization that *mathematically guarantees* convergence. Define $\phi = c_1 + c_2$ and the constriction factor:

$$\chi = \frac{2}{\vert \phi - 2 \vert + \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi}}$$

The velocity update becomes $\mathbf{v}_i(t+1) = \chi\!\left[\mathbf{v}_i(t) + c_1 r_1 (\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{x}_i) + c_2 r_2 (\mathbf{g} - \mathbf{x}_i)\right]$.

For $\phi = 4.1$ ($c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$), $\chi \approx 0.729$. This single multiplier effectively damps the system and prevents the "explosion" of particles, while still allowing the algorithm to explore early and converge later.

#### (b) Compare PSO with Genetic Algorithm (GA). List at least four advantages and two limitations of PSO. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | PSO | GA |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Inspiration | Social behaviour of swarms | Biological evolution (natural selection) |
| Representation | Real-valued vectors | Real or binary strings (chromosomes) |
| Operators | Velocity + position update | Selection, crossover, mutation |
| Memory | Each particle retains pbest & gbest | No explicit memory of past generations |
| Information sharing | All particles share gbest (global communication) | Through the population (chromosome mixing) |
| Parameters | $w, c_1, c_2, N$ | Population size, crossover rate, mutation rate |
| Number of parameters | Few (3–4) | More (typically 4–5) |
| Convergence speed | Generally faster (uses gbest sharing) | Slower (relies on stochastic operators) |
| Premature convergence | Higher risk (gbest pulls all) | Lower risk (mutation introduces diversity) |
| Gradient requirement | None | None |
| Parallelism | Inherent (particles evolve simultaneously) | Inherent (population-based) |

**Advantages of PSO:**
1. **Simplicity** — only a few lines of code; few parameters to tune.
2. **Derivative-free** — does not require gradient information; applicable to non-differentiable and black-box functions.
3. **Faster convergence** — gbest sharing accelerates the search compared to GA's stochastic operators.
4. **Memory** — each particle remembers pbest, allowing it to recover from bad moves.
5. **Population-based** — less likely to get stuck at a single point than local-search methods.

**Limitations of PSO:**
1. **Premature convergence** — the swarm can be trapped in local optima when gbest is a local minimum.
2. **Lack of diversity mechanism** — unlike GA's mutation, standard PSO has no operator to introduce new genetic material; particles may cluster.
3. **Parameter sensitivity** — performance is sensitive to $w, c_1, c_2$ which may require problem-specific tuning.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students LOSE marks in PSO questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to update pbest before using it in the next iteration's velocity update.** pbest is *historical best* — it does NOT change to the current $x_i$ every iteration; it only changes when the current position is *better*. Marks are lost when students set $\mathbf{p}_i = \mathbf{x}_i$ unconditionally.
> 2. **Using $\mathbf{p}_i$ in the cognitive term instead of $(\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{x}_i)$** — the formula is a *difference vector*, not the pbest position itself.
> 3. **Confusing pbest with gbest** — pbest is *per particle*; gbest is *per swarm*. Examiners specifically check this distinction.
> 4. **Forgetting random numbers $r_1, r_2$** — without them, the algorithm becomes deterministic and loses its stochastic exploration property. Always include $r_1, r_2 \sim U(0,1)$.
> 5. **Not stating the role of inertia weight** — when asked "explain the velocity update", students often write the equation but skip the *interpretation* of $w, c_1, c_2$. KTU's outcome-based evaluation demands this.
> 6. **In worked numerical problems, computing pbest updates after position update but failing to use the *new* pbest in gbest computation for the same iteration** — sequence is critical: pbest update → gbest update → velocity update.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PSO is a population-based, gradient-free, stochastic, meta-heuristic algorithm** inspired by social behaviour of bird flocks and fish schools.
- The **velocity update** is a linear combination of three forces: inertia ($w\mathbf{v}$), cognitive pull ($c_1 r_1 (\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{x})$), and social pull ($c_2 r_2 (\mathbf{g} - \mathbf{x})$).
- The **position update** is simply $\mathbf{x}_i(t+1) = \mathbf{x}_i(t) + \mathbf{v}_i(t+1)$.
- **pbest** is the best position *a single particle* has ever visited; **gbest** is the best position *any particle* has ever visited.
- For **convergence** of standard PSO, the necessary condition is $0 \le w < 1$ and $0 < c_1 + c_2 < 4(1 + w)$.
- **Clerc & Kennedy's constriction coefficient** ($\chi \approx 0.729$ for $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$) is an alternative parameterization that *mathematically guarantees* convergence.
- **Linearly decreasing inertia weight** (LDIW) from $0.9$ to $0.4$ is the most common tuning strategy to balance exploration and exploitation.
- **gbest PSO** converges faster but is more prone to premature convergence; **lbest PSO** is slower but more diverse and better at avoiding local optima.
- Compared to **GA**, PSO is simpler (fewer parameters), has a built-in memory (pbest), and typically converges faster, but suffers more from premature convergence.
- PSO is widely used in **power system optimization, control tuning, neural network training, structural design, and feature selection** — quote one application in your answer for full marks.
- Always state the **assumptions** (minimization), **parameter values** ($w, c_1, c_2$), and **random number draws** ($r_1, r_2$) in numerical problems.
- **Termination criteria** include: maximum iterations, fitness tolerance, stall generation limit, or computational budget.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute exam tip:** Memorize the canonical velocity update equation *word-for-word*, and prepare a 4-line "PSO in 4 steps" mnemonic: **Initialize → Evaluate → Update pbest & gbest → Update velocity & position**. This alone earns you 5–6 marks in any PSO question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ant Colony Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT637 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ant Colony Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 : Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)

## 1. Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** is a **population-based, probabilistic metaheuristic** inspired by the real-world foraging behavior of biological ants. It belongs to the broader class of **Swarm Intelligence** algorithms and is used to find approximate solutions to complex combinatorial optimization problems. The algorithm uses a colony of artificial "ants" that construct candidate solutions by probabilistically traversing a **construction graph**, guided by **pheromone trails** and **heuristic information**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a colony of real ants searching for the shortest path from their nest to a food source.

1. **Blind Following**: Real ants are practically blind. While walking, they deposit a chemical substance called **pheromone** on the ground.
2. **Pheromone Trail**: When an ant reaches a fork in the path, it is more likely to choose the path that has a **stronger pheromone concentration**. This positive feedback loop means that shorter paths accumulate pheromone faster because ants traverse them more frequently within a fixed time.
3. **Evaporation**: Old pheromone trails evaporate over time, preventing the algorithm from getting stuck in a suboptimal path (a process called **stagnation**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Mapping**: In engineering optimization (e.g., finding the shortest route for a delivery truck, routing data packets in a network, or designing a circuit), the "food source" is the optimal solution, and the "ants" are computational agents exploring the design space.

### Standard Metrics in ACO

* **$\tau_{ij}$**: Pheromone intensity on the edge/path connecting node $i$ to node $j$.
* **$\eta_{ij}$**: Heuristic desirability (often $1/d_{ij}$, where $d_{ij}$ is the distance/cost).
* **$\alpha$**: Weight controlling the influence of the pheromone trail.
* **$\beta$**: Weight controlling the influence of the heuristic information.
* **$\rho$**: Pheromone evaporation rate, where **$0 < \rho < 1$**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operational Concept Breakdown

The ACO algorithm operates in an iterative loop with three primary phases:

1. **Solution Construction (Ant Movement)**: Each ant builds a solution by moving from node to node. The probability of ant $k$ moving from node $i$ to node $j$ is governed by the **Probabilistic Transition Rule**.
2. **Pheromone Update (Evaporation & Deposition)**: After all ants have constructed their solutions, the pheromone trails are updated. This involves two steps:
   * **Evaporation**: A constant fraction of the existing pheromone is evaporated to allow exploration.
   * **Deposition**: Ants that found better solutions deposit a higher amount of pheromone along their paths.
3. **Daemon Actions (Optional)**: In more advanced ACO variants (like **MAX-MIN Ant System - MMAS**), a daemon process applies additional local search or updates only the best-so-far ant to intensify the search.

### Why and How it Works
* **The "Why" of Positive Feedback**: Short paths get traversed faster, getting more pheromone deposits per unit time. This natural bias ensures the algorithm converges towards optimality.
* **The "How" of Evaporation**: Without evaporation, the initial random fluctuations in pheromone would rigidify. Evaporation acts as a forgetting mechanism, allowing the algorithm to "forget" bad decisions and explore new areas.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula Name** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Variables & Constraints** | **Engineering Utility** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Transition Probability** | $$P_{ij}^k(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^\alpha \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^\beta}{\sum_{l \in N_i^k} [\tau_{il}(t)]^\alpha \cdot [\eta_{il}]^\beta}$$ | $N_i^k$ = set of unvisited nodes | Determines the stochastic path choice for the artificial ant. |
| **Heuristic Information** | $$\eta_{ij} = \frac{1}{d_{ij}}$$ | $d_{ij}$ = cost/distance of edge $i \to j$ | Provides domain-specific knowledge (a priori). |
| **Pheromone Evaporation** | $$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t)$$ | $0 < \rho < 1$ (evaporation rate) | Prevents early convergence to local optima. |
| **Pheromone Deposition** | $$\Delta\tau_{ij}^k = \frac{Q}{L_k}$$ | $L_k$ = tour length of ant $k$; $Q$ = constant | Rewards better, shorter paths with more pheromone. |
| **Global Update Rule** | $$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^m \Delta\tau_{ij}^k$$ | $m$ = total number of ants | Total pheromone intensity for the next iteration. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parameter Tuning Note**: If **$\alpha=0$**, the algorithm ignores pheromone and acts as a standard greedy heuristic. If **$\beta=0$**, only pheromone amplification occurs, leading to rapid, often suboptimal, convergence.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation: From Biological Behavior to Mathematical Transition Rule

### Step 1: Defining the Problem Domain
Consider a **Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP)** where an artificial ant must visit $n$ cities exactly once and return to the start. We define a fully connected construction graph $G = (C, L)$, where $C$ is the set of cities and $L$ is the set of edges connecting them.

### Step 2: Establishing the Probabilistic Choice
At any node (city) $i$, the ant $k$ must choose the next city $j$ from its feasible neighborhood $N_i^k$. The ant's choice is not deterministic but a probability function dependent on two key factors: the **pheromone trail** ($\tau_{ij}$) representing learned desirability, and the **heuristic value** ($\eta_{ij}$) representing immediate cost savings.

### Step 3: Formulating the Multiplicative Combination
To combine these factors, we use a weighted product, as addition is inadequate for representing the compounding nature of chemical signals.

$$
\text{Numerator} = [\tau_{ij}]^\alpha \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^\beta
$$

Here, exponents $\alpha$ and $\beta$ determine the relative importance of past experience versus current distance.

### Step 4: Normalization over the Feasible Set
To convert the raw product into a valid probability distribution (where the sum of all choices equals 1), we divide by the sum of the products over all allowed next steps.

$$
P_{ij}^k(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^\alpha \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^\beta}{\sum_{l \in N_i^k} [\tau_{il}(t)]^\alpha \cdot [\eta_{il}]^\beta}
$$

*Explanation:* The denominator $\sum_{l \in N_i^k}$ acts as a normalization constant, ensuring $0 \leq P_{ij}^k(t) \leq 1$.

### Step 5: Pheromone Update Rule Derivation
After constructing a full tour, ant $k$ deposits pheromone. The total pheromone on edge $(i, j)$ at time $t+1$ is calculated by taking what was there at time $t$, evaporating a fraction $\rho$, and adding the new deposits.

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \Delta\tau_{ij}
$$

If we expand $\Delta\tau_{ij}$ for an ant system (AS), it is defined as $\frac{Q}{L_k}$ where $L_k$ is the length of the tour constructed by ant $k$.

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \frac{Q}{L_k}
$$

## Numerical Illustration: TSP Update Calculation

**Scenario**: Let us track a single edge between City A and City B.
* Initial Pheromone $\tau_{AB}(t) = \mathbf{10}$
* Evaporation Rate $\rho = \mathbf{0.1}$
* Tour length of ant $k$ $L_k = \mathbf{20}$ units.
* Constant $Q = \mathbf{100}$

**Calculation Logic**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{AB}(t+1) &= (1 - 0.1) \cdot (10) + \frac{100}{20} \\
\tau_{AB}(t+1) &= (0.9)(10) + 5 \\
\tau_{AB}(t+1) &= 9 + 5 = \mathbf{14}
\end{aligned}
$$

The pheromone intensity on the path increased from $10$ to $14$, making it more attractive to subsequent ants.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## ACO Algorithm Flowchart (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StartA([Initialize Parameters: alpha, beta, rho, m ants]) --> InitP[Initialize Pheromone Matrix tau_ij]
    InitP --> PlaceAnts[Place m ants on random cities]
    PlaceAnts --> BuildSol{For each ant k: Construct Solution}
    BuildSol --> ProbChoice[Select next city using Probability P_ijk]
    ProbChoice --> CheckVis[Check visited cities list]
    CheckVis --> BuildSol
    BuildSol --> EvalSol[Evaluate total tour cost L_k]
    EvalSol --> EvapStep[Pheromone Evaporation: tau = 1-rho times tau]
    EvapStep --> DepositStep[Pheromone Deposition: Add Q over L_k]
    DepositStep --> IterCheck{Iteration less than MaxIter?}
    IterCheck -- Yes --> BuildSol
    IterCheck -- No --> OutputBest[Output Global Best Tour]
    OutputBest --> StopA([End Algorithm])
```

## ACO Pheromone Update Cycle Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Ant Tour Inputs]
        A[Tour Length Lk]
        B[Pheromone Decay rho]
        C[Constant Q]
    end

    subgraph ComputeLayer[Update Engine]
        D[Evaporation Module: 1 - rho]
        E[Deposition Module: Q divided by Lk]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[Pheromone State]
        F[New Pheromone tau_ij t+1]
    end

    A --> E
    B --> D
    C --> E
    D --> F
    E --> F
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Ant Colony Optimization. List its two main influencing parameters.**
**Model Answer**:
Ant Colony Optimization is a population-based metaheuristic algorithm inspired by the foraging behavior of real ants, used to solve combinatorial optimization problems. The two main influencing parameters that guide an artificial ant's path construction are:
1. **Pheromone Intensity ($\tau_{ij}$)**: Represents the accumulated experience of the colony.
2. **Heuristic Information ($\eta_{ij}$)**: Represents the a priori desirability, typically the inverse of distance ($\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$).

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Explain the role of pheromone evaporation in ACO.**
**Model Answer**:
Pheromone evaporation is controlled by the parameter $\rho$ ($0 < \rho < 1$). Its role is to decrease the pheromone level on all edges by a factor of $(1-\rho)$ at each iteration. This mechanism prevents premature convergence to suboptimal paths (stagnation), allowing the algorithm to "forget" poor decisions and explore new possibilities in the search space.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each - Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**(a)** Explain the biological inspiration of the Ant Colony Optimization algorithm in detail. (7 Marks)
**(b)** Describe the algorithmic steps of ACO for the Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP), including the transition probability rule and the global pheromone update rule. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution**:

**(a) Biological Inspiration:**
Real ants in nature deposit a chemical substance called **pheromone** on the paths they traverse. While navigating, they probabilistically prefer paths with higher pheromone concentrations. Because ants traveling via the shortest route take less time, they complete more trips, thereby depositing more pheromone on the short path within a given time frame. This creates a positive feedback loop where the shortest path eventually accumulates the strongest pheromone trail, guiding the entire colony. Additionally, pheromone naturally evaporates over time.

**(b) Algorithmic Steps for TSP:**
1. **Initialization**: Set parameters $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\rho$. Initialize pheromone trails $\tau_{ij}(0)$ to a small constant $c$.
2. **Solution Construction**: Place $m$ ants randomly on $n$ cities. Each ant builds a tour by repeatedly applying the transition probability rule:
$$
P_{ij}^k(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^\alpha \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^\beta}{\sum_{l \in N_i^k} [\tau_{il}(t)]^\alpha \cdot [\eta_{il}]^\beta}
$$
3. **Evaluation**: Compute the length $L_k$ of the tour constructed by each ant $k$.
4. **Pheromone Update**: Perform global update to alter pheromone levels. First, evaporation occurs on all edges:
$$
\tau_{ij}(t) \leftarrow (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t)
$$
Second, ants deposit pheromone on their traversed edges:
$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^m \frac{Q}{L_k}
$$
5. **Termination**: Repeat Steps 2-4 until a maximum number of iterations is reached, then output the best tour found.

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**(a)** Compare and contrast the Ant System (AS) and the MAX-MIN Ant System (MMAS). (7 Marks)
**(b)** A salesman must visit 4 cities A, B, C, D and return to A. Given initial pheromone $\tau = 1$ for all edges, $\alpha=1$, $\beta=2$, $\rho=0.1$, and distance matrix $d = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 2 & 4 & 5 \\ 2 & 0 & 1 & 3 \\ 4 & 1 & 0 & 6 \\ 5 & 3 & 6 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$, calculate the probability of an ant moving from City A to City B using the ACO transition rule. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution**:

**(a) Comparison of AS and MMAS:**
* **Pheromone Deposition**: In the standard **Ant System (AS)**, all ants deposit pheromone upon completing a tour. In the **MAX-MIN Ant System (MMAS)**, pheromone deposition is restricted; typically, only the iteration-best or global-best ant is allowed to deposit pheromone to intensify the search.
* **Pheromone Limits**: MMAS introduces explicit minimum ($\tau_{min}$) and maximum ($\tau_{max}$) bounds on the pheromone values. AS has no such limits, which can lead to stagnation if one path accumulates disproportionately high pheromone.
* **Initialization**: MMAS often initializes pheromone to the upper bound $\tau_{max}$ to favor initial exploration, whereas AS initializes to a small arbitrary constant.

**(b) Numerical Calculation:**
Given parameters: $\tau_{AC} = \tau_{AD} = \tau_{AB} = \mathbf{1}$, $\alpha=1$, $\beta=2$.
Distances from City A: $d_{AB} = \mathbf{2}$, $d_{AC} = \mathbf{4}$, $d_{AD} = \mathbf{5}$.
Heuristic values ($\eta = 1/d$): $\eta_{AB} = 0.5$, $\eta_{AC} = 0.25$, $\eta_{AD} = 0.2$.

Numerator for A to B:
$$
[\tau_{AB}]^1 \cdot [\eta_{AB}]^2 = (1) \cdot (0.5)^2 = \mathbf{0.25}
$$

Numerator for A to C:
$$
[\tau_{AC}]^1 \cdot [\eta_{AC}]^2 = (1) \cdot (0.25)^2 = \mathbf{0.0625}
$$

Numerator for A to D:
$$
[\tau_{AD}]^1 \cdot [\eta_{AD}]^2 = (1) \cdot (0.2)^2 = \mathbf{0.04}
$$

Denominator (Sum of all options):
$$
0.25 + 0.0625 + 0.04 = \mathbf{0.3525}
$$

Probability of moving from A to B:
$$
P_{AB} = \frac{0.25}{0.3525} \approx \mathbf{0.7092}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: 
> Students frequently lose marks by failing to square the heuristic term $\eta_{ij}$ because of the $\beta=2$ parameter. Always remember to raise BOTH $\tau_{ij}$ and $\eta_{ij}$ to their respective alpha and beta powers. Additionally, do not forget to include the pheromone evaporation factor $(1-\rho)$ during the update phase; examiners specifically check this term in long derivations.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **Origin**: ACO was introduced by **Marco Dorigo** in his Ph.D. thesis in 1992.
* **Algorithm Class**: It is a **metaheuristic** (high-level strategy) and **constructive** (builds solutions step-by-step).
* **Core Mechanism**: Positive feedback via pheromone accumulation and negative feedback via evaporation.
* **Key Parameters**: $\alpha$ (pheromone influence), $\beta$ (heuristic influence), $\rho$ (evaporation rate), $m$ (number of ants), $Q$ (pheromone constant).
* **Applications**: TSP, Vehicle Routing, Graph Coloring, Network Routing, Scheduling.
* **Variants**: **Ant System (AS)**, **Ant Colony System (ACS)**, **MAX-MIN Ant System (MMAS)**.
* **Convergence**: Stagnation is a risk; mitigated by evaporation and bounded limits (MMAS).
* **Trade-off**: High $\alpha$ leads to early convergence; high $\beta$ makes it greedy but potentially locally optimal.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Matlab/Scilab to Solve Optimization Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT637 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Use of Matlab/Scilab to Solve Optimization Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Computational Optimization** refers to the systematic application of numerical software environments—primarily **MATLAB (Matrix Laboratory)** and its open-source counterpart **Scilab**—to model, solve, and analyze mathematical programming problems. Within the KTU 2024 scheme for *Optimization Techniques* (PEECT637), this topic specifically deals with translating theoretical formulations of **Linear Programming (LP)**, **Non-Linear Programming (NLP)**, **Integer Programming (IP)**, and **Quadratic Programming (QP)** into executable code using high-level solver functions and toolbox routines.

The core objective is to leverage built-in **Numerical Algorithms** (such as the Simplex method, Interior-Point method, Active-Set method, Sequential Quadratic Programming, and Branch-and-Bound) so that students can solve multi-variable, multi-constraint real-world problems without manually performing tedious matrix operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course outcome (CO) for Module 4 emphasizes *"solving complex optimization problems using standard software tools and interpreting the results."* Students must know the syntax of the **solver function**, the **meaning of the output structure**, and the **interpretation of exit flags**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Master Chef's Recipe Book" Analogy

Imagine you are a **master chef** who has perfected recipes (the *mathematical theory* of optimization) but needs a **smart kitchen assistant** (MATLAB/Scilab) to:
1. Read the recipe (define the objective function $f(x)$ and constraints $g(x), h(x)$),
2. Measure every ingredient to gram-level precision (handle large matrices and floating-point arithmetic),
3. Cook the dish in parallel using multiple stoves (execute iterative numerical algorithms),
4. And finally, **plate and present** the final dish in a clear, structured format (return the optimal decision variables $x^*$, objective value $f(x^*)$, and a status report).

**MATLAB/Scilab is that smart kitchen assistant.** The chef (the student) only needs to write the *recipe in a standard format* (proper function syntax), and the assistant handles the heavy computational lifting.

## 1.3 Why Software Tools are Indispensable in Modern Optimization

| Aspect | Manual Method (Graphical / Simplex Table) | MATLAB / Scilab Method |
|---|---|---|
| Problem Size | Max ~3 variables, ~5 constraints | Hundreds/thousands of variables & constraints |
| Time Required | Hours (for medium problems) | Seconds to minutes |
| Error Rate | High (arithmetic mistakes) | Very Low (machine-precision arithmetic) |
| Visualization | Limited to 2D/3D | 3D surface plots, contour plots, convergence graphs |
| Algorithm Variety | Single (usually Simplex) | Simplex, Interior-Point, SQP, GA, PSO, etc. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Convention used in this Module:**
> - Decision variable vector: $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$
> - Objective function: $\min f(x)$ or $\max f(x)$
> - Inequality constraints: $A \cdot x \leq b$
> - Equality constraints: $A_{eq} \cdot x = b_{eq}$
> - Bounds: $lb \leq x \leq ub$
> - Solver exit flag: $exitflag = 1$ (converged) or $exitflag = 0$ (did not converge)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Optimization Problem Formulation

Every problem solved in MATLAB/Scilab is first cast into the **standard form** that the solver understands. For minimization problems, this is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{x} \quad & f(x) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & c(x) \leq 0 \\
& c_{eq}(x) = 0 \\
& lb \leq x \leq ub
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Convention:** All MATLAB/Scilab solvers (linprog, fmincon, intlinprog, quadprog) are written for **minimization**. To solve a maximization problem, we apply the transformation: $\max f(x) \equiv \min -f(x)$.

## 2.2 The Master Tool: `linprog` (Linear Programming)

`linprog` is the workhorse for **Linear Programming** problems. The problem it accepts is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{x} \quad & f^T x \\
\text{subject to} \quad & A \cdot x \leq b \\
& A_{eq} \cdot x = b_{eq} \\
& lb \leq x \leq ub
\end{aligned}
$$

### KTU Exam-Relevant Syntax (MATLAB)

```matlab
[x, fval, exitflag, output] = linprog(f, A, b, Aeq, beq, lb, ub, options)
```

### KTU Exam-Relevant Syntax (Scilab)

```scilab
// Using the 'linpro' function (older Scilab versions)
// Or 'optim' for newer Scilab
[x, fval, exitflag] = linpro(f, A, b, Aeq, beq, lb, ub)
```

## 2.3 The Master Tool: `fmincon` (Nonlinear Programming)

`fmincon` handles problems where $f(x)$ or the constraints are **nonlinear**. It accepts a problem defined either via an **objective function file** or via **symbolic expressions** (using the problem-based approach introduced in MATLAB R2017b).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{x} \quad & f(x) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & c(x) \leq 0 \\
& c_{eq}(x) = 0 \\
& A \cdot x \leq b, \quad A_{eq} \cdot x = b_{eq} \\
& lb \leq x \leq ub
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** `fmincon` requires an **initial guess** $x_0$. The quality of the solution is highly sensitive to this guess for non-convex problems.

## 2.4 Complete KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Problem Type | MATLAB Solver | Scilab Solver | Algorithm Used | Returns |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Programming (LP) | `linprog` | `linpro` / `karmarkar` | Simplex, Interior-Point | $x$, $fval$, $exitflag$ |
| Integer Linear Programming (ILP) | `intlinprog` | `intlinpro` (via FOSS) | Branch-and-Bound | $x$, $fval$, $exitflag$ |
| Quadratic Programming (QP) | `quadprog` | `quapro` | Active-Set, Interior-Point | $x$, $fval$, $\lambda$ |
| Non-Linear Programming (NLP) | `fmincon` | `optim` / `fminsearch` | SQP, Interior-Point, Trust-Region | $x$, $fval$, $\lambda$ |
| Unconstrained NLP | `fminunc` | `fminsearch` | Quasi-Newton, Nelder-Mead | $x$, $fval$ |
| Multi-Objective | `gamultiobj` | External toolbox | NSGA-II | Pareto front |
| Global (Black-Box) | `ga`, `particleswarm`, `simulannealbnd` | External | Genetic, PSO, SA | $x$, $fval$ |

### 2.4.1 The Master Syntax Reference

| Input Argument | Mathematical Meaning | KTU Notation |
|---|---|---|
| `f` | Coefficients of linear objective $f^T x$ | Vector of size $n \times 1$ |
| `A`, `b` | Inequality constraints $A x \leq b$ | Matrix $m \times n$, vector $m \times 1$ |
| `Aeq`, `beq` | Equality constraints $A_{eq} x = b_{eq}$ | Matrix $p \times n$, vector $p \times 1$ |
| `lb`, `ub` | Lower and upper bounds on $x$ | Vectors of size $n \times 1$ |
| `x0` | Initial guess (NLP only) | Vector of size $n \times 1$ |
| `nonlcon` | Nonlinear constraint function (NLP) | Function handle returning $[c, c_{eq}]$ |
| `options` | Solver configuration (algorithm, tolerance, iterations) | `optimoptions` struct |
| `exitflag` | Convergence status | $1$ = converged, $0$ = max iters, $-2$ = infeasible, $-3$ = unbounded |

## 2.5 The Output Structure — What Each Returned Value Means

| Output | Meaning | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| `x` | Optimal decision variables | The "best" production plan, route, dosage, etc. |
| `fval` | Objective function value at $x^*$ | Minimum cost, maximum profit, minimum time |
| `exitflag` | Termination status | Did the algorithm find a valid solution? |
| `output` | Diagnostic info (iterations, algorithm used, message) | Useful for debugging and reporting |
| `lambda` | Lagrange multipliers (NLP/QP) | Shadow prices, sensitivity information |

## 2.6 Algorithm Selection — Engineering Wisdom

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Concept:** *"Which algorithm should I choose in `options`?"*

1. **For LP**: Use `'Interior-Point'` for large sparse problems (default in modern MATLAB); use `'Dual-Simplex'` for warm-starting sensitivity analysis.
2. **For NLP**: Use `'sqp'` (Sequential Quadratic Programming) for small-to-medium smooth problems; use `'interior-point'` for large-scale problems; use `'trust-region-reflective'` when bounds are active.
3. **For global optimization**: Use `ga` (Genetic Algorithm) or `particleswarm` when the objective is **non-differentiable, discontinuous, or has many local minima**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Linear Programming in MATLAB (KTU-Style)

### Problem Statement
A KTU exam classic: *A company manufactures two products P1 and P2. Each unit of P1 yields a profit of ₹40 and each unit of P2 yields ₹30. The company has 240 hours of labor and 100 kg of raw material. P1 requires 4 hours of labor and 2 kg of material; P2 requires 3 hours of labor and 1 kg of material. Formulate as LP and solve using MATLAB.*

### Step 1 — Mathematical Formulation

Let $x_1$ = units of P1 produced, $x_2$ = units of P2 produced.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max \quad & Z = 40 x_1 + 30 x_2 \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & 4 x_1 + 3 x_2 \leq 240 \quad \text{(Labor)} \\
& 2 x_1 + 1 x_2 \leq 100 \quad \text{(Material)} \\
& x_1, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert to Standard `linprog` Form (Minimize)

Since `linprog` minimizes, we negate the objective:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min \quad & -40 x_1 - 30 x_2 \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & 4 x_1 + 3 x_2 \leq 240 \\
& 2 x_1 + 1 x_2 \leq 100 \\
& x_1, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Identify Matrix Inputs

$$
f = \begin{bmatrix} -40 \\ -30 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
A = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 3 \\ 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
b = \begin{bmatrix} 240 \\ 100 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
lb = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4 — Complete MATLAB Code

```matlab
% --- KTU Module 4 : Linear Programming using linprog ---
clc; clear;

% Step 4.1: Define the objective coefficient vector (for MINIMIZATION)
f = [-40; -30];

% Step 4.2: Define inequality constraints A*x <= b
A = [4 3;
     2 1];
b = [240; 100];

% Step 4.3: No equality constraints -> pass [] for Aeq, beq
Aeq = [];
beq = [];

% Step 4.4: Lower bounds (non-negativity)
lb = [0; 0];

% Step 4.5: No explicit upper bound
ub = [];

% Step 4.6: Call the solver
[x, fval, exitflag, output] = linprog(f, A, b, Aeq, beq, lb, ub);

% Step 4.7: Display results
fprintf('Optimal Production Plan:\n');
fprintf('  x1 (P1 units) = %.2f\n', x(1));
fprintf('  x2 (P2 units) = %.2f\n', x(2));
fprintf('Maximum Profit     = ₹%.2f\n', -fval);
fprintf('Exit Flag          = %d\n', exitflag);
```

### Step 5 — Expected Output

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= 30, \quad x_2 = 40 \\
Z_{\max} &= 40(30) + 30(40) = 1200 + 1200 = \text{₹}2400 \\
\text{exitflag} &= 1 \quad \text{(Converged)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Verification (Manual Cross-Check)

Substitute $x_1 = 30, x_2 = 40$ into constraints:
- Labor: $4(30) + 3(40) = 120 + 120 = 240 \leq 240$ ✓ (Binding)
- Material: $2(30) + 1(40) = 60 + 40 = 100 \leq 100$ ✓ (Binding)

Both constraints are **tight (binding)**, which is a property of an optimal basic feasible solution (BFS) when $n = m = 2$.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Nonlinear Programming using `fmincon`

### Problem Statement
Minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1 - 2)^2 + (x_2 - 3)^2$ subject to $x_1 + x_2 = 4$.

### Step 1 — Mathematical Analysis

The unconstrained minimum is at $(2, 3)$ with $f = 0$, but this violates the equality constraint. The constrained optimum lies on the line $x_1 + x_2 = 4$ closest to $(2, 3)$.

### Step 2 — Formulate the Constraint as $c_{eq}(x) = 0$

$$
c_{eq}(x) = x_1 + x_2 - 4 = 0
$$

### Step 3 — Write the Objective Function File (`objfun.m`)

```matlab
function f = objfun(x)
    f = (x(1) - 2)^2 + (x(2) - 3)^2;
end
```

### Step 4 — Write the Nonlinear Constraint File (`nonlconfun.m`)

```matlab
function [c, ceq] = nonlconfun(x)
    c   = [];            % No inequality nonlinear constraints
    ceq = x(1) + x(2) - 4;   % Equality constraint
end
```

### Step 5 — Main Script

```matlab
% --- KTU Module 4 : NLP using fmincon ---
clc; clear;
x0 = [1; 1];   % Initial guess
options = optimoptions('fmincon', 'Display', 'iter', 'Algorithm', 'sqp');
[x, fval, exitflag, output, lambda] = ...
    fmincon(@objfun, x0, [], [], [], [], [], [], @nonlconfun, options);
disp(x), disp(fval)
```

### Step 6 — Expected Output (Analytical Verification)

Using Lagrange multipliers: $\nabla f = \lambda \nabla g$ gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2(x_1 - 2) &= \lambda \\
2(x_2 - 3) &= \lambda \\
x_1 + x_2 &= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting the first two: $2(x_1 - x_2) + 2 = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 = x_2 - 1$. Substituting into the constraint:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x_2 - 1) + x_2 &= 4 \\
2 x_2 &= 5 \\
x_2 &= 2.5, \quad x_1 = 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

The optimal point is $x^* = (1.5, 2.5)$ with $f(x^*) = (1.5-2)^2 + (2.5-3)^2 = 0.25 + 0.25 = 0.5$.

The solver returns `exitflag = 1` and `lambda.ineqnonlin` shows the Lagrange multiplier value.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Integer Programming using `intlinprog`

### Problem Statement (Classical KTU Problem)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min \quad & 5 x_1 + 4 x_2 \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & 6 x_1 + 4 x_2 \geq 24 \\
& x_1 + 2 x_2 \geq 6 \\
& x_1, x_2 \geq 0 \text{ and integer}
\end{aligned}
$$

### MATLAB Code

```matlab
f = [5; 4];
intcon = [1, 2];   % Both x1 and x2 must be integers
A = [-6 -4; -1 -2];  % Negate to convert '>=' to '<='
b = [-24; -6];
lb = [0; 0];
[x, fval] = intlinprog(f, intcon, A, b, [], [], lb);
```

### Expected Output

$$
x_1 = 2, \quad x_2 = 3, \quad f_{\min} = 5(2) + 4(3) = 22
$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Scilab Equivalent of LP

In Scilab, the same Example 1 is solved using the `linpro` function (older versions) or `optim` module:

```scilab
// KTU Module 4 : Linear Programming in Scilab
clc; clear;
f = [-40; -30];
A = [4 3; 2 1];
b = [240; 100];
lb = [0; 0];
ub = [%inf; %inf];
[x, fval, exitflag] = linpro(f, A, b, [], [], lb, ub);
disp(x, 'Optimal x =');
disp(-fval, 'Maximum Profit =');
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference:** In Scilab, `%inf` represents $+\infty$ (no upper bound), whereas in MATLAB the equivalent is `inf` (without the `%`).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Symptom in Problem | Recommended Solver | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| All $f$, $A$, $b$ are linear | `linprog` | Simplex/Interior-Point is exact & fast |
| Some $x_i$ must be integers | `intlinprog` | Branch-and-Bound rounds solutions |
| Objective has $\frac{1}{2} x^T H x$ term | `quadprog` | Specialized for QP, very efficient |
| $f(x)$ or $g(x)$ is nonlinear | `fmincon` | Uses gradient-based SQP or IP methods |
| No constraint, smooth $f$ | `fminunc` | Quasi-Newton is faster than `fmincon` |
| Black-box, non-differentiable, multimodal | `ga` / `particleswarm` | Global search, no gradient needed |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The MATLAB/Scilab Optimization Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Read the Word Problem] --> B[Step 2: Identify Decision Variables xi]
    B --> C[Step 3: Formulate Objective f x and Constraints g x, h x]
    C --> D{Step 4: Classify Problem Type}
    D -->|Linear| E[Use linprog / linpro]
    D -->|Nonlinear Smooth| F[Use fmincon / optim]
    D -->|Quadratic| G[Use quadprog / quapro]
    D -->|Integer Variables| H[Use intlinprog]
    D -->|Black-Box Multimodal| I[Use ga or particleswarm]
    E --> J[Step 5: Build A, b, Aeq, beq, lb, ub Matrices]
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Step 6: Call Solver Function]
    K --> L[Step 7: Receive Output x, fval, exitflag, lambda]
    L --> M{Step 8: Check exitflag}
    M -->|exitflag = 1| N[Step 9a: Converged - Validate and Report]
    M -->|exitflag = 0| O[Step 9b: Max Iterations - Increase Iterations in options]
    M -->|exitflag = -2| P[Step 9c: Infeasible - Re-check Constraints]
    M -->|exitflag = -3| Q[Step 9d: Unbounded - Check Objective Direction]
    N --> R[Step 10: Generate Plots and Engineering Report]
    O --> R
    P --> R
    Q --> R
```

## 4.2 The Layered Architecture of an Optimization Solver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UIL[User Input Layer]
        A1[Problem Definition]
        A2[Initial Guess x0]
        A3[Solver Options]
    end

    subgraph PFL[Problem Formulation Layer]
        B1[Objective Function f x]
        B2[Linear Constraints A, b, Aeq, beq]
        B3[Nonlinear Constraints nonlcon]
        B4[Bounds lb, ub]
    end

    subgraph NAL[Numerical Algorithm Layer]
        C1[Simplex Method]
        C2[Interior-Point Method]
        C3[Active-Set Method]
        C4[Sequential QP]
        C5[Branch-and-Bound]
    end

    subgraph SOL[Solver Core]
        D1[Pre-Processing Scaling and Sparsity]
        D2[Iterative Computation]
        D3[Convergence Check]
    end

    subgraph OUL[Output Layer]
        E1[Optimal x]
        E2[Optimal fval]
        E3[exitflag and lambda]
        E4[Diagnostic Messages]
    end

    UIL --> PFL
    PFL --> NAL
    NAL --> SOL
    SOL --> OUL
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Have an Optimization Problem] --> Q1{Is the objective linear?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Are all variables continuous?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q5
    Q2 -->|Yes| LP[Use linprog]
    Q2 -->|No| ILP[Use intlinprog]
    Q5{Is the objective quadratic?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| QP[Use quadprog]
    Q5 -->|No| Q6{Is f smooth and differentiable?}
    Q6 -->|Yes| NLP[Use fmincon]
    Q6 -->|No, black-box| GO[Use ga or particleswarm]
    LP --> End[Ready to Code]
    ILP --> End
    QP --> End
    NLP --> End
    GO --> End
```
